headlines.title: FIGHT MADE ON PAVING
headlines.subtitle: Hemphill Street Work May Be Stopped Because Water Mains Are Not Laid.
headlines.fullText:
Paving of Hemphill street south from Betty streets to the city limita may be held up for more than a year as a result of the city commission's ac- Butter Compte tweemoden stan Hanke's motion for an opinion on stop- Hanke said it would be an outrage for the commission to permit the pay. 5 .se eren of the street and every one of the commons ought to be recalled if it were per- mitted
"A city ordinance says there shall be a lead service before paving be- Jos." Blanke said, "but the ordinance makes no provision for paying for the lead service. The property owners of- fred to pay for the lead service on one year's time, but we turned their prop. Sebe we will be in any better condition to may this a year from now than we are
"We might let the paving go on by serait devine ways and means for ming in the water main before the putting In street is already traveled and work will start soon that will make the laying of water main Ampcasible unless we stop the work Bw. I want the city attorney's opin- lin as to whether we can stop the werk after it has been ordered.
Cost of Main $3.000.
The cost of the main will be about 1,000. The waterworks department bas no money for this work. The city attorney is to report on this question at the next regular commission meet-
Commissioner Blanke protested that the waterworks department Is uremer that the water mains are laid improp- wy. He asked for an ordinance re- spring the city engineer to pass upon ejery water main before paving begins city attorney was Instructed to craft such an ordinance

headlines.title: BELT ROAD IS LOSER IN 2 DAMAGE SUITS
headlines.subtitle: $25,000 Verdict Given Result of Auto Accident of January 10.
headlines.fullText:
Quick Judgment for damages was given Tuesday in two suits against the Fort Worth Belt Railway Company re- sulting from the automobile accident of Jan. 10. 1314. in which four persons were killed. The judgment in each of the two suits was for $12,500. The cases were heard before Judge Buck without
Maggie B. MeLemore, widow of E. E. Mclemore, one of the men killed, her four children. F.F. Melemore je 1 . Lemore and John D' Mellemers and she was joined in the sutt by Robert H. und Caroline Melamore parents of E. Mclemore. The widow was award- ed $7,500 and the children $1,250 each. Judgment was rendered for the de- Tendant company against the plaintiffs. Robert H. and Caroline Mclemore.
Ethel Kelly Corley, widow of Eugene time, also asked $25,000 on behalf of herself and her daughter. Gwendolyn in the suit by Mr. John H. Corley, de Huet decided in favor child, of the widow and chill, but refused to Count of the judgment, $7.500 is to 20 to the widow and $5,000 to the child.

headlines.title: Domestic Science Teacher Takes Up Work at Y. W.C.A.
headlines.fullText: Miss Ethel Ollis, new domestic aci- ence teacher at the Y. W. C. A., took active charge of the cafeteria conduct- ed by the association Tuesday. Miss Ollis is a graduate of the University of esta her teacher in teria are being lengthcafe rection. Beginnich: Oct. 1. she will con- duet classes in the principles of cook Ing and In chafing dish cooking and candy making. A housekeeper's conk- Ing clara will be organized and a course In dietetics probably will be added later in the fall.

headlines.title: PUBLICHEARING ON MILK LAW SEPTEMBER 9

headlines.title: ROTARIANSLAY BIG PLANS FOR FAT-LEAN GAME
headlines.subtitle: Parade Will Form at 2:30, Sept. 11 to Go to Park for Fight to Finish.
headlines.fullText:
Forty to fifty Rotarians attended the luncheon Tuesday.
Details of plans for the Rotary Club field day and baseball game between the Fats and the Leans, to be held at Morris Park Sept. 11 for the benefit of Cumberland fest, were outlined to the general committee Tuesday at a luncheon at the Westbrook hotel which The committee on tickets has had G dispone of every one of them.
Plans for a parade have been made composed of L. P. Robertson. C. B. Brown. J. IL. Overstreet. W. W. Henkel and Hamer B. Lyne. The parade will consist of a truck carrying the band, truck-a large one carrying the members of the Fat team, a truck-or buggy carrying the members of the Lean toam, automobiles carrying the members of the Rotary Club and their wives and the women compris ta board of the Cumberland Rest. The parade will form at 2:36 P. m. manas er nane theater lemon station cific union station, turning north on Stain street there and going straight on to Morris Park.

headlines.fullText:
Bushy Busby Funeral services of J. L. Bus. manufacturers agent of Fort se hela from the residence. 1431 Pennsylvania avenue, at 19 a. m. Tuesday. The body, accompanied by Mrs. Busby and others, arrived over the Santa Fe Monday.
Gann-L. G. Gann, 64, died Tuesday morning at his residence, 1117 Stelln street. The body will be sent to May. pearl Wednesday morning by the Fort Worth Undertaking Company.

headlines.title: MINE LEADERS FOUND GUILTY OF CONTEMPT
headlines.subtitle: Seven Persons, Including Peter R. Stewart, Given Jail Sentences.
headlines.fullText:
LITTLE ROCK. Ark, Sept. 1 .- Seven members of the United Mine Workers of America, Including Peter R. Stew- art, who recently resigned as preste dent of the district union composed of Arkansas, Oklahoma and Texaso- day were found guilty of contempt of court by band zoneneed Fort Smith, and sentenced to serve from thirty days to four months in jall
The charges grew out of at the, coas men the men sentenced months in prison. to four mowe charged with being the ted in the riots which occurred after the operators had obatined an in- junction.

headlines.title: ASKS $20,000 FOR DEATH OF HUSBAND
headlines.subtitle: Northern Texas Traction Company Sued by Mrs. Emma
headlines.fullText: Mrs. Emma Atkins filed suit in dis- triet court Monday for $20,000 against the Northern Tesis man depend- Fun and Other ape that sam Ana Fumar on the Fort Worth-Dallas interurban, April 26. 1913. Mrs. Atkins claims $5.000; Mrs. Audie Browning, a daughter of Atkins. claims $5,000, and Mrs. Atkins asks $10.000 for her son. The petition alleges Atkins fell against an interurban car as a result of the roughness of the ground alone" alde mar efterbeen ir- and no lights had been placed to ffi- sure the safety of passengers, it Is claimed.
